title: Sam Holt
content:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  (Air: ן¿½Ben Bolt.ן¿½)


ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Oh! donן¿½t you remember Black Alice, Sam Holtן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Black Alice, so dusky and dark,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The Warrego gin, with the straw through her nose,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And teeth like a Moreton Bay shark.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The terrible sheepwash tobacco she smoked
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In the gunyah down there by the lake,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And the grubs that she roasted, and the lizards she stewed,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And the damper you taught her to bake.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Oh! donן¿½t you remember the moonן¿½s silver sheen,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And the Warrego sand-ridges white?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And donן¿½t you remember those big bull-dog ants
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ We caught in our blankets at night?

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Oh! donן¿½t you remember the creepers, Sam Holt,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That scattered their fragrance around?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And donן¿½t you remember that broken-down colt
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ You sold me, and swore he was sound?

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And donן¿½t you remember that fiver, Sam Holt,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ You borrowed so frank and so free,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ When the publican landed your fifty-pound cheque
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ At Tambo your very last spree?

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Luck changes some natures, but yours, Sammy Holt,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Was a grand one as ever I see,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And I fancy Iן¿½ll whistle a good many tunes
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Ere you think of that fiver or me.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Oh! donן¿½t you remember the cattle you duffed,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And your luck at the Sandy Creek rush,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And the poker you played, and the bluffs that you bluffed,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And your habits of holding a flush?

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And donן¿½t you remember the pasting you got
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ By the boys down in Callaghanן¿½s store,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ When Tim Hooligan found a fifth ace in his hand,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And you holding his pile upon four?

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ You were not the cleanest potato, Sam Holt,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ You had not the cleanest of fins.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But you made your pile on the Towers, Sam Holt,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And that covers the most of your sins.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ They say youן¿½ve ten thousand per annum, Sam Holt,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In England, a park and a drag;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Perhaps you forget you were six months ago
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In Queensland a-humping your swag.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But whoן¿½d think to see you now dining in state
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With a lord and the devil knows who,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ You were flashing your dover, six short months ago,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In a lambing camp on the Barcoo.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Whenן¿½s my time coming?ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Perhaps never, I think,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And itן¿½s likely enough your old mate
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Will be humping his drum on the Hughenden-road
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To the end of the chapter of fate.
meaning:
This evocative piece by Banjo Paterson (Andrew Barton), titled "Sam Holt", represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance...

### Why We Love This Line
At Linespedia, we believe that poetry is the ultimate sanctuary for the soul...
